charts.title: Topic Map
charts.description: High-level overview of fuzzy logics: motivation, the three standard semantics, validity, the Intermediate Value Theorem, isomorphism, and Trillas' Theorem.

nodes.label:
Week 5
Fuzzy Logics
nodes.note: Week 5 generalises classical truth values from {0,1} to the unit interval [0,1] to support graded reasoning. Three standard fuzzy logics are studied along with the deep result Trillas' Theorem on negation.

nodes.label:
Motivation
(Sorites)
nodes.note: The sorites paradox shows that classical logic cannot model gradual properties like baldness or heaps. Fuzzy logic introduces real-valued truth degrees in [0,1].

nodes.label:
Truth
Functions
nodes.note: Boolean truth functions on {0,1}^n are generalised to fuzzy truth functions on [0,1]^n. A fuzzy model V assigns a real number in [0,1] to each variable.

nodes.label:
Three Fuzzy
Logics
nodes.note: F1 Min-Max (Zadeh), F2 Product (Goedel), and F3 Lukasiewicz logics differ in how they interpret and, or, and implication, but all agree on negation = 1 - x.

nodes.label: Validity
nodes.note: F is valid in a fuzzy logic if its truth function returns 1 for every choice of valuations in [0,1]. Many classical tautologies fail in some fuzzy logics.

nodes.label:
Intermediate
Value Theorem
nodes.note: The IVT is a classical analysis result used throughout fuzzy logic, especially to prove that any reasonable negation has a fixed point c with not(c) = c.

nodes.label:
Ordered
Isomorphism
nodes.note: Two ordered structures are isomorphic if there is a bijection that preserves both the order and all the functions. This is the formal notion behind Trillas' Theorem.

nodes.label:
Trillas'
Theorem
nodes.note: Any negation satisfying four reasonable properties N1-N4 is isomorphic to 1 - x. This explains why all standard fuzzy logics agree on negation.

nodes.label: F1 (Min-Max)
nodes.note: Zadeh's logic uses and = min, or = max, and Lukasiewicz-style implication. Or is idempotent in F1.

nodes.label: F2 (Product)
nodes.note: Goedel's product logic uses arithmetic product for and and probability sum for or. Idempotency of and and or both fail.

nodes.label: F3 (Lukasiewicz)
nodes.note: Lukasiewicz logic uses bounded sum for or and bounded difference for and. It is the only one of the three where excluded middle and non-contradiction hold.

nodes.label:
Negation
Properties N1-N4
nodes.note: A reasonable negation is Boolean (N1), involutive (N2), continuous (N3), and decreasing (N4). These four properties characterise the operator up to isomorphism.

nodes.label:
Fixed
Point of neg
nodes.note: By N1 and N3, the function f(x) = not(x) - x changes sign on [0,1], so by IVT there is some c with not(c) = c. This c plays a central role in Trillas' construction.

charts.title: Evaluating a Fuzzy Formula
charts.description: Procedure for computing the truth value of a propositional formula in any of the three standard fuzzy logics.

nodes.label:
Formula F and
valuation V
nodes.note: We are given a propositional formula F together with a fuzzy valuation V mapping each variable to a real number in [0,1].

nodes.label:
Choose fuzzy logic
F1, F2, or F3
nodes.note: Pick which fuzzy semantics to evaluate in. Each logic has its own truth functions for and, or, and implication, but they all agree on negation = 1 - x.

nodes.label:
Parse F into
subformulas
nodes.note: Decompose F into its sub-formulas down to the propositional variables. The truth value will be computed bottom-up by composing truth functions.

nodes.label:
Substitute V(p)
for each variable p
nodes.note: Replace every propositional variable in the parse tree with the real number assigned by the valuation V. The leaves of the tree now hold concrete values in [0,1].

nodes.label:
Apply truth functions
bottom-up
nodes.note: For every connective, evaluate it using the truth function from the chosen fuzzy logic. Continue until you reach the root of the parse tree.

nodes.label:
Get value
v in [0,1]
nodes.note: The result is a real number in [0,1] representing the degree of truth of F under V in the chosen fuzzy logic.

nodes.label: v equal to 1?
nodes.note: Check whether the resulting value is exactly 1. A value of 1 means the formula is fully true; any value below 1 means the formula is partially true under V.

nodes.label:
V is a model
of F
nodes.note: Since V(F) = 1 in the chosen logic, the valuation V is a model of F. This is the fuzzy analogue of classical satisfaction.

nodes.label:
V is NOT
a model of F
nodes.note: The truth degree v is less than 1, so V is not a model of F. The valuation may also serve as a counter-example to validity.

charts.title: Three Fuzzy Logics - Connective Comparison
charts.description: Taxonomy of the truth functions for the three standard fuzzy logics F1, F2, and F3.

nodes.label:
Standard Fuzzy
Logics
nodes.note: Three fuzzy logics share the same truth functions for negation but differ on the others. Each captures a different intuition about partial truth.

nodes.label:
F1 Min-Max
(Zadeh)
nodes.note: Uses min for and, max for or, with Lukasiewicz-style implication. Or is idempotent here, but and and or do not satisfy excluded middle.

nodes.label:
F2 Product
(Goedel)
nodes.note: Uses arithmetic product for and and probability sum for or. Implication is the ratio y over x when x > y.

nodes.label: F3 Lukasiewicz
nodes.note: Uses bounded sum for or and bounded difference for and. The only one where excluded middle and non-contradiction always hold.

nodes.label:
Shared
Negation
nodes.note: All three logics use neg(x) = 1 - x. Trillas' Theorem explains why this is essentially forced for any reasonable negation.

nodes.label: and = min(x,y)
nodes.note: F1 uses the minimum of the two truth values for conjunction. This is the strongest and operation: very pessimistic.

nodes.label: or = max(x,y)
nodes.note: F1 uses the maximum of the two truth values for disjunction. This makes or idempotent in F1.

nodes.label: and = x * y
nodes.note: F2 uses the arithmetic product. Repeated conjunction with values less than 1 quickly drives the truth degree towards zero.

nodes.label: or = x + y - x*y
nodes.note: F2 uses the probability sum, which is the inclusion-exclusion formula. Or is not idempotent in F2.

nodes.label: and = max(0,x+y-1)
nodes.note: F3 uses bounded difference. This is the only operator that drives a-and-not-a to exactly 0, validating the law of non-contradiction.

nodes.label: or = min(1,x+y)
nodes.note: F3 uses bounded sum. This is the only operator that drives a-or-not-a to exactly 1, validating the law of excluded middle.

charts.title: Trillas' Theorem - Proof Structure
charts.description: Step-by-step structure of the proof of Trillas' Theorem that any reasonable negation is isomorphic to 1 - x.

nodes.label:
Negation neg
satisfies N1-N4
nodes.note: We begin from the assumption that the negation neg has the four desirable properties: Boolean, involutive, continuous, and decreasing.

nodes.label: Define f(x) = neg(x) - x
nodes.note: Construct the helper function f. By N1 we have f(0) = 1 > 0 and f(1) = -1 < 0, so f changes sign on [0,1].

nodes.label:
Apply IVT
on f
nodes.note: Because neg is continuous (N3), so is f. The Intermediate Value Theorem then guarantees a point c in [0,1] where f(c) = 0.

nodes.label:
Get fixed point
neg(c) = c
nodes.note: From f(c) = 0 we get neg(c) - c = 0, so c is a fixed point of neg. This c will anchor the isomorphism construction.

nodes.label:
Define rho(x):
 piecewise around c
nodes.note: Define rho(x) = x/(2c) for x <= c and rho(x) = 1 - neg(x)/(2c) for x >= c. This piecewise function maps [0,1] bijectively to [0,1].

nodes.label:
Verify rho is
order-preserving bijection
nodes.note: Both pieces are continuous and strictly increasing, agree at x = c, and map endpoints 0 -> 0 and 1 -> 1. Hence rho is an order-preserving bijection.

nodes.label:
Show rho(neg(x))
= 1 - rho(x)
nodes.note: Split into the cases x <= c and x > c. In each case, use N4 to show neg(x) lies on the opposite side of c, then evaluate rho on both sides.

nodes.label:
rho is the desired
isomorphism
nodes.note: The bijection rho preserves order and satisfies rho(neg(x)) = 1 - rho(x), so the structure ([0,1], <, neg) is isomorphic to ([0,1], <, 1-x).

charts.title: Verifying a Negation Function (N1-N4)
charts.description: Decision procedure for checking that a candidate function satisfies the four reasonableness properties of a fuzzy negation.

nodes.label:
Candidate
negation neg
nodes.note: We are given a candidate function neg : [0,1] -> [0,1] and want to verify whether it qualifies as a reasonable fuzzy negation.

nodes.label:
N1: neg(0)=1
and neg(1)=0?
nodes.note: Substitute the endpoints into the candidate. Both must hold for the function to extend the classical Boolean negation.

nodes.label:
Not a valid
negation
nodes.note: At least one of N1 to N4 has failed, so the candidate is not a reasonable fuzzy negation. It need not be isomorphic to 1 - x.

nodes.label:
N2: neg(neg(x))=x
for all x?
nodes.note: Plug neg(x) into neg again and simplify symbolically. The result must equal x identically on [0,1] for involutivity to hold.

nodes.label:
N3: neg is
continuous?
nodes.note: Check continuity using either the epsilon-delta definition, the composition rule, or by observing that the function is built from elementary continuous parts.

nodes.label:
N4: neg is
decreasing?
nodes.note: Show that x < y implies neg(x) >= neg(y), either by computing the derivative or by direct algebraic comparison of fractions.

nodes.label:
neg satisfies
N1-N4
nodes.note: All four properties hold, so by Trillas' Theorem the structure ([0,1], <, neg) is isomorphic to ([0,1], <, 1-x).
